column5: I bought a purdy necklace. (That's right. I said purdy. People always question me whenever I use that term.) It's a Celtic symbol for an iris
column6: the same symbol as the one on the Quebec flag. Here's what it said on the bit of cardboard it came on:        ""FLUER DE LEIS: The Fluer De Leis is the sign of the House of Bourbon
column9: a member in every royal house in Europe. 'The Flower of Leis' is still found on the standards of several ruling families
column10: including that of Spain. It is actually an iris
column11: and is most associated with France.""        Now
column13: I'm not all that emotionally attached to France or European politics. However
column14: this is such a nice necklace that I felt I should have some sort of story to tell people when they ask about it. To help me come up with such a story
column15: I did about 5 minutes of online research on iris' (irises?) and came up with the following:     ""Well you see
column16: I like to think of the iris as a flower with similar characteristics to an avid go-getter. For example
column19: the far North...pretty much everywhere. As people
column20: it's a good idea for us to also explore new surroundings and adapt to unfamiliar environments. As a second point
column22: and there's a pot of gold at the end of every rainbow
column23: so I figure that those with similar qualities to the iris in matters of perseverance and adaptability will be rewarded in the future.""        Yeah yeah
column24: I know it's a smart-ass thing to say. It's usually adults who ask about things like that though
column25: and they're usually impressed with children actually having something intelligent to say at their parents' get-togethers."
